title: Handover for Multi-Beam LEO Satellite Networks: A Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning Method

variables.p_{i,l,v}: transmit power between user i and beam (l,v)
variables.G_{i,l,v}(t): channel gain between user i and beam (l,v) at time t

sinrFormulaDetail.notes: SNR formula (no interference term). Channel gain includes path loss, atmospheric fading, and Rician small-scale fading.

channelModelDetail.atmosphericModel: Cloud and rain attenuation modeled via exponential distance-altitude factor

algorithmDetail.stateSpace: s_i_t = (u_i(t), G_i(t), Gamma(t), N(t)) — access vector, channel gains to all beams, beam locations, number of users per beam
algorithmDetail.actionSpace: a_i_t = (u_{i,1,1}, ..., u_{i,L,V}) — binary access vector selecting one beam, sum = 1
algorithmDetail.rewardFunction: Vector reward R_i_t = (r1, r2, r3): r1 = throughput of user i; r2 = -phi1 (intra-sat HO) or -phi2 (inter-sat HO) or 0; r3 = -(max beam throughput - min beam throughput)/I
algorithmDetail.networkArchitecture: Three parallel DQN networks (one per objective), each with 3 hidden layers (100, 50, 50 neurons), tanh activation
algorithmDetail.convergence: Trained over episodes; linear scalarization of Q-values for action selection; adapts to different preference weights without retraining

handoverProcedure: 1) Each user agent observes state (access vector, channel gains, beam locations, beam loads). 2) Agent computes scalarized Q-value ζ = Ω^T · Q(s,a) across three DQN networks. 3) Action selected via ε-greedy: a = argmax ζ(s,a). 4) User connects to the selected beam (l,v). 5) If selected beam differs from previous, handover occurs (intra-satellite if same satellite, inter-satellite otherwise). 6) Environment returns vector reward and transitions to next state.

keyEquations.name: Channel Gain
keyEquations.purpose: Compute channel quality between user and beam

keyEquations.name: SNR
keyEquations.purpose: Signal quality metric for rate calculation

keyEquations.name: Transmission Rate

variables.N_{l,v}(t): number of users served by beam

keyEquations.purpose: Per-user achievable rate with equal bandwidth sharing

keyEquations.name: Scalarized Q-value
keyEquations.purpose: Combine multiple objective Q-values for action selection

performanceMetrics: weighted reward (weighted sum of three objective rewards)
keyFindings: With weights [0.5, 0.3, 0.2], MODQN achieves the best trade-off among throughput, handover cost, and load balancing compared to RSS_max, DQN_throughput, and DQN_scalar across varying user counts (40-200), satellite counts (2-8), user speeds (30-150 km/h), and satellite speeds (7.0-7.8 km/s). MODQN adapts to different preference scenarios via one-time training, avoiding the retraining overhead of traditional scalar-reward DQN approaches.

orbital.constellation: custom LEO constellation built in STK

user.area: 200 km x 90 km rectangular area centered at (40°N, 116°E)

baselineDetails.name: RSS_max
baselineDetails.description: Selects the beam with the best channel quality (maximum received signal strength)

baselineDetails.name: DQN_throughput
baselineDetails.description: Traditional single-objective DQN that only maximizes throughput as scalar reward

baselineDetails.name: DQN_scalar
baselineDetails.description: Traditional DQN with a weighted scalar reward combining three objectives

numericalResults.improvement: notable improvement across all user counts
numericalResults.conditions: 4 satellites, 30 km/h user speed, 7.4 km/s satellite speed, weights [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]
100 users, 30 km/h user speed, 7.4 km/s satellite speed

numericalResults.improvement: better trade-off; DQN_throughput slightly better on throughput alone but worse on HO cost and load balancing

systemModelAssumptions: Each user can only access one beam at a time (single connectivity)
Equal bandwidth sharing among users within the same beam
Intra-satellite handover cost is lower than inter-satellite handover cost (0 < phi1 < phi2)
Channel gain composed of FSPL, atmospheric fading, and Rician small-scale fading (no interference)
Users move with random wandering mobility model
Satellites run in fixed orbits at constant speed (STK-based)

powerAnalysis.transmitPower: p_{i,l,v} = 2 W (transmit power per beam-user link)
powerAnalysis.powerControl: No dynamic power control; fixed 2 W per link
powerAnalysis.notes: SNR-based (no interference); power is simple fixed parameter

geometryFormulas.slantRange: d_{i,l,v}(t) — distance between user i and beam (l,v); implicit in channel gain formula, no explicit closed-form
geometryFormulas.elevationAngle: Not explicitly modeled; paper does not include elevation angle in formulas
geometryFormulas.atmosphericFading: A(d) = 10^{3 d \chi / (10 h)}, \chi = 0.05 dB/km atmospheric attenuation

beamModel.bandwidthPerBeam_MHz: 500 / N_{l,v}(t) shared among users in beam
beamModel.notes: Intra-satellite HO (beam switch) costs less than inter-satellite HO

linkDirection: not explicitly stated (SNR formula implies downlink from satellite to user)

dapsSupport.notes: Single connectivity; user accesses one beam at a time

energyEfficiency.notes: Not an objective; focuses on throughput, HO cost, and load balancing

relevanceToResearch.direction: DRL for Energy-Efficient Beam Configuration and DAPS-Assisted Seamless Handover in Multi-Beam LEO Satellite Networks
keyContributions: Multi-objective DRL for multi-beam LEO handover — directly applicable framework
Distinguishes intra-satellite (beam) vs inter-satellite handover costs
Multi-objective Q-learning with linear scalarization — avoids retraining for different preference weights
Three parallel DQN networks for three objectives — architectural reference
7 beams per satellite with equal BW sharing
gaps: No beam pattern or off-axis angle modeling

jsSimulationNotes.channelModel: SNR = p*G/sigma^2; G = (c/(4pi*d*fc))^2 * A(d) * eta. Rician fading eta, atmospheric A(d) = 10^(3d*chi/(10h)). Simple to implement.
jsSimulationNotes.orbitModel: STK-generated positions for 4 satellites at 780 km; or generate with 7.4 km/s circular orbit
jsSimulationNotes.keyParameters: 20 GHz, 500 MHz BW, 2 W power, N0=-174 dBm/Hz, K=20 dB Rician, 0.05 dB/km atm, 7 beams/sat, 100 users in 200x90 km, 30 km/h user speed
jsSimulationNotes.complexityNotes: Three separate DQN networks (100-50-50, tanh) per user agent; action = select one beam from all visible beams. 9000 episodes, 10s episodes.

limitations: No interference modeling — uses SNR instead of SINR
Small constellation (2-8 satellites) — scalability to mega-constellations not validated
Centralized training assumed — each user has an independent agent but no coordination mechanism
Only linear scalarization explored for combining objectives — Pareto front not computed

sinrAndSignal.notes: Paper provides a SNR formula rather than an explicit SINR formula.

power.notes: SNR-based (no interference); power is simple fixed parameter
